scenario: Forge Insurance runs Camunda 8.8 for an underwriting platform with 2200 policies per day. A senior operator is preparing a checklist for retiring old decision definitions and wants to recall the exact risk that the userguide highlights when deleting the only existing version of a decision definition from the Decisions page.
question: Which risk does the Delete decision definition from Decisions Page note specifically associate with deleting the only existing version of a decision definition?

options.text: Deleting the only existing version of a decision definition could result in process incidents on running process instances that still reference that decision in their model
Deleting the only existing version of a decision definition silently switches all dependent process instances to a hard-coded default outcome on the cluster, with no incident raised to the operator
Deleting the only existing version of a decision definition forces a 24 hours read-only window on the surrounding DRD before any other operation can be performed in Operate
Deleting the only existing version of a decision definition requires the operator to deploy a replacement DRD within 10 minutes from Modeler, otherwise Operate auto-restores the deleted version

a.text: Correct. The userguide note states that deleting the only existing version of a decision definition could result in process incidents, because dependent process instances no longer find a decision to evaluate against on the cluster.

b.text: Incorrect. The docs do not describe a silent default outcome; the documented risk is that the deletion could result in process incidents.

c.text: Incorrect. There is no documented 24 hours read-only window after deletion; the docs flag the risk of process incidents instead.

d.text: Incorrect. The userguide does not describe a 10 minutes replacement window or an auto-restore behaviour; the call-out is about the risk of process incidents.

explanation: Inside the same note that explains DRD-level impact, the userguide highlights a second risk: deleting the only existing version of a decision definition could result in process incidents. This makes it explicit that operators should keep at least one usable version of a decision definition available on the cluster.
